text: The video begins with the repair of an immediate colorectal leak using transanal laparoscopy, following its discovery during an abdominal test after a laparoscopic colorectal anastomosis.
The surgeon introduces the transanal reusable D-port into the anus, preparing for transanal laparoscopy.
The surgeon applies a silicon cap with three orifices to the transanal reusable D-port, preparing for further transanal laparoscopic procedures.
The surgeon uses a 10mm, 30-degree rigid scope of regular length to visualize the surgical field transanally.
A monocurved grasping forceps is introduced through the orifice at the nine o'clock position of the transanal D-port.
The surgeon inserts a monocurved needle holder into the orifice at the three o'clock position, following the introduction of the suture through the transanal reusable D-port.
The surgeon places a suture on the staple line at the level of the defect and begins the repair process, using the transanal approach with the D-port in place.
The surgeon performs multiple suture bites using a monocurved needle holder through the orifices of the silicon cap, visible through the ten millimeter, thirty degree scope.
The surgeon completes the first repair by tying an intraluminal knot, maintaining ergonomic positions throughout the procedure.
The surgeon uses monocurve scissors to cut the suture within the surgical field, completing the repair process.
The surgeon begins a new suture on the staple line at the defect site, using a monocurved needle holder to guide the suture placement.
The surgeon performs collision-free interluminal work, ensuring smooth manipulation within the lumen without interference from surrounding structures.
The surgeon ties an interluminal knot ergonomically, completing the procedure.

The second part of this video shows a transanal laparoscopic repair of a late colorectal fistula in a patient submitted four weeks before a laparoscopic anterior resection of the rectum with temporary ileostomy.
The surgeon introduces the transanal reusable D-port into the anus, preparing for further surgical access and manipulation.
The surgeon exposes the colorectal fistula, revealing the affected tissue area within the rectal cavity.
The surgeon introduces a monocurve grasping forceps at the nine o'clock position and a monocurve needle holder at the three o'clock position through the transanal D-port orifice.
The surgeon places the first suture on the inferior edge of the defect and passes it inside the defect, initiating the repair process.
The surgeon passes the suture through the superior edge of the defect, completing the placement of the suture within the exposed colorectal fistula.
The surgeon performs a figure-of-eight suture by passing the suture a second time through the tissue at the site of the colorectal fistula.
The surgeon uses scissors inserted through the orifice at the three o'clock position to cut off the needle, following the exposure of the colorectal fistula.
The surgeon ties an interluminal knot within the rectal defect using previously placed sutures, securing the tissue edges together.
The surgeon performs another figure-of-eight suture using the same technique, followed by tying a final intracorporeal knot to secure the suture.
The final view of the repair shows the surgical site with a clear lumen, indicating no stenosis present.
Immediate per-operative complications during abdominal laparoscopic colorectal surgery, such as a leak, late colorectal complications, such as a fistula, can be solved using the transanal approach.
This new transanal platform offers the surgeon good working ergonomics and does not increase the cost of the procedure thanks to reusable nature of the materials adopted.
